npm.README.md.desc: The main npm README.md for the tskb package
npm.README.md.content:
# tskb Let your **AI assistant document your codebase** as it works. Architecture knowledge accumulates in a **compiler-verified artifact** — query it, ask questions about your system, navigate it with your AI. > **Your AI writes the docs. You navigate them.** [![npm version](https://badge.fury.io/js/tskb.svg)](https://www.npmjs.com/package/tskb) [![License: MIT](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-MIT-blue.svg)](https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT) [Repository](https://github.com/mitk0936/tskb) · [Issues](https://github.com/mitk0936/tskb/issues) · [npm](https://www.npmjs.com/package/tskb) --- ## Quick start ```bash npm install --save-dev tskb npx tskb init # interactive — pass --yes to accept all defaults npm run docs # added to package.json by `init` npx tskb explore ``` An empty graph, an interactive D3 explorer in the browser, and AI skills wired into Claude Code and GitHub Copilot. The codebase map fills in as you (and your AI) write `.tskb.tsx` docs over time. --- ## Status **Beta** — `0.7.x`. - **Stable:** CLI commands (`init`, `build`, `ls`, `pick`, `search`, `docs`, `flows`, `context`, `explore`), JSX components, registry primitives, graph schema, generated skill/instruction files. - **May still evolve:** internal extraction APIs, explorer chunk format, additional node types. Pin a minor version if you script against the JSON output. --- ## Preview ![tskb explorer](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mitk0936/tskb/main/references/explorer.png) --- ## What is tskb? A TypeScript-native DSL for expressing **architectural intent** as code. Your AI writes and maintains documentation in `.tskb.tsx` files that are **type-checked by the TypeScript compiler** and reference **real folders, files, and exports** via `typeof import()`. The result is a **structured knowledge graph** you can query with the CLI, browse in the explorer, or feed back to your AI — all from the same source of truth. > Refactor your code → stale docs break at compile time. The AI fixes them. --- ## Why it exists Large TypeScript codebases drift in predictable ways: - **Tribal knowledge** — architecture lives in senior engineers' heads - **Documentation decay** — Markdown and diagrams silently fall out of sync - **Compound complexity** — onboarding cost grows faster than the codebase tskb makes architecture documentation **typed, validated, and enforced at build time** — so it can't quietly rot. --- ## Core concepts ### Vocabulary Declare structural elements once in the global `tskb` namespace. Six primitives cover the surface: | Primitive | What it represents | Validation | | ---------- | ------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------- | | `Folder` | A logical area (feature, layer, package) | `path` must exist on disk | | `Module` | A source file, with type-safe access to its shape | `typeof import("…")` | | `Export` | A named export (class, fn, const) | `typeof import("…").Symbol` | | `File` | A non-TS/JS file (README, config, spec) | `path` must exist on disk | | `Term` | A domain concept not tied to a specific file | string literal type | | `External` | A third-party service or package | free-form key-value props | ```tsx // docs/vocabulary.tskb.tsx import type { Folder, Module, Export, Term } from "tskb"; declare global { namespace tskb { interface Folders { ServiceLayer: Folder<{ desc: "Business logic"; path: "src/services" }>; } interface Modules { AuthServiceModule: Module<{ desc: "Authentication service"; type: typeof import("../src/services/auth.service.js"); }>; } interface Exports { AuthService: Export<{ desc: "Authentication and authorization"; type: typeof import("../src/services/auth.service.js").AuthService; }>; } interface Terms { jwt: Term<"JSON Web Tokens for stateless authentication">; } } } ``` `typeof import()` means the compiler validates every reference. Rename `AuthService` → the doc build breaks. ### Documents `.tskb.tsx` files reference the vocabulary and add prose, snippets, flows, relations, and decisions. JSX components: ``, `

` / `

` / `

`, `

`, `` / `

  • `, ``, `` / ``, ``, ``. ```tsx // docs/authentication.tskb.tsx import { Doc, H1, P, Snippet, ref } from "tskb"; import { AuthService } from "../src/services/auth.service.js"; // `ref` is a typed sentinel — the cast tells TS which registered node this identifier represents. const Service = ref as tskb.Exports["AuthService"]; const Jwt = ref as tskb.Terms["jwt"]; export default (

    Authentication

    {Service} issues {Jwt} after validating credentials.

    { const auth = new AuthService(); return auth.login({ email: "user@example.com", password: "secret" }); }} />     ); ``` `` is **not** a string literal — its body is fully type-checked. If `login()` changes signature, the doc build fails. ### Flows Named, ordered sequences through the system — login pipelines, build steps, request paths. They become first-class graph nodes. ```tsx // AuthMiddleware, AuthService, and UserRepository are declared in your vocabulary // the same way AuthService is declared above, then pulled in via `ref as tskb.Exports[...]`. ``` ### Relations and ADRs Express explicit semantic edges between any two nodes, and capture the _why_ behind decisions: ```tsx

    Stateless tokens let us scale the auth tier horizontally without sticky sessions.

     ``` ### Priority Three levels control what the AI sees first: - `essential` — surfaced in generated AI skills and `ls` output - `constraint` — architectural rules; surfaced in `pick`/`search` so they can't be missed before changes - `supplementary` — graph-only, queryable on demand --- ## How the graph is built Edges come from two sources: **Authored intent** — every JSX reference creates a typed edge. `

    {AuthService} uses {UserRepository}

    ` records edges for both. `` adds an explicit `related-to` edge. **Inferred structure** — folder hierarchy, module imports, export ownership, and file containment are read from the filesystem and TypeScript AST. The result combines what you _meant_ with what's _actually there_. --- ## CLI ```bash npx tskb init # Interactive scaffolder (--yes for defaults) npm run docs # Build the graph (script added by init) npx tskb build "" --project --tsconfig # Direct build invocation npx tskb ls --depth 4 # Folder tree + essential docs npx tskb pick "AuthService" # Full detail for one node npx tskb search "auth" # Fuzzy search across the graph npx tskb context "ServiceLayer" # Node + neighborhood + docs (one call) npx tskb docs "auth" # Search documentation npx tskb flows # List flows by priority npx tskb explore # Open the interactive explorer npx tskb explore --export ./public # Bake static site ``` All commands accept `--plain` (~30% fewer tokens for AI consumption) and `--optimized` (compact JSON). `context` is the most efficient single call — it returns the target node, its neighborhood, and all referencing docs in one shot. --- ## Explorer `tskb explore` serves a D3-powered SPA that renders the graph as an interactive diagram — folders nest, modules expand, edges trace references and imports. Ships pre-built; no extra install. `--export` writes a self-contained static folder you can drop on any host. --- ## AI assistant integration The build emits skills/instructions for Claude Code and GitHub Copilot, regenerated from the graph on every build — never edit the files manually. | Skill | Purpose | | ---------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **tskb** | Codebase map and CLI orientation. Always-on entry point. | | **tskb-toc** | Repo table of contents: boundaries, externals, flows, essential-docs index. Loaded on demand. | | **tskb-update** | When and where to write `.tskb.tsx` docs — workflow, folder structure, authoring rules. | | **tskb-update-syntax** | Full authoring syntax reference: registry primitives, JSX components, snippets, class methods. | Enable them during `npx tskb init` or create `.claude/skills/` and `.github/` before the next build. ### How it compounds At the start of a session the assistant already has the folder tree and essential docs. For anything deeper it runs `search`, `context`, or `pick` itself. Each session adds to the graph; the next session sees what's already there. > **You:** "I'm adding JWT auth in `src/services/auth.service.ts`. Document it." > > **AI** (via `tskb-update`): > > 1. Runs `tskb search "auth"` and `tskb context "src/services"` — checks what's already mapped. > 2. Proposes the questions worth answering: _"How does login issue a JWT?"_, _"What guarantees token expiry?"_ > 3. You pick which questions are real. The AI declares new vocabulary and writes one `.tskb.tsx` per question. > 4. Runs `npm run docs`. The compiler validates every reference and snippet. > > **Next session:** the AI finds the auth doc via `tskb search`, references `AuthService` directly — no re-reading the code. --- ## Output schema ```ts { nodes: { folders, modules, exports, files, terms, flows, docs // typed records }, edges: Array<{ from: string; to: string; type: "references" | "contains" | "belongs-to" | "imports" | "related-to" | "flow-step"; label?: string; }>, metadata: { generatedAt, version, stats } } ``` The graph is the primary output. The explorer and AI skill files are all derived from it. --- ## How it differs **vs Markdown / ADRs** — type-checked against real code, not free text that drifts. **vs Structurizr / C4 / PlantUML** — native TypeScript instead of a custom DSL; produces a queryable graph, not just static diagrams. **vs TypeDoc** — documents _architecture and intent_ (why things exist, how they relate), not API surfaces. Unique to tskb: compiler-validated references via `typeof import()`, type-checked code snippets, first-class flows, doc priority, an interactive explorer, and AI skills generated from the graph. --- ## Roadmap - Architectural constraint validation (beyond `priority="constraint"` surfacing) - **First-class JSX for common architecture patterns** — purpose-built components for ``, ``, ``, ``, ``, ``, and pipeline / saga / pub-sub variants. Each emits typed graph nodes and edges, surfaces violations at build time, and renders distinctly in the explorer. - More AI assistant integrations --- ## License MIT © Dimitar Mihaylov
